Principle: Track diagnostic episodes over time, not only encounters.
Why it matters: Repeated visits and unresolved symptoms were repeatedly documented as black zones.
Design implication: Create an unresolved-problem layer across visits, tests, referrals, messages and patient check-ins.

Principle: Separate acuity sorting from diagnostic uncertainty.
Why it matters: Front-door workflows sort urgency; hidden/chronic/complex issues can remain unexplained even when not emergent.
Design implication: Show both urgency and unresolved diagnostic risk; do not let low acuity equal resolved diagnosis.

Principle: Preserve patient voice without making it the final diagnosis.
Why it matters: Patient complaints reveal cut-off histories, dismissal and positive patterns of being listened to.
Design implication: Store patient’s own words, daily impact and top fear separately from clinician assessment; allow corrections.

Principle: Every result/referral/pending test needs an owner, deadline and closure state.
Why it matters: Abnormal-result and referral follow-up failures recur across patient-safety, clinician and complaint evidence.
Design implication: Build ledgers with owner, backup, due date, patient notification, action, and escalation.

Principle: Normal tests must be paired with symptom status and test limitations.
Why it matters: Normal-test traps appeared across ED, primary care, specialist and patient-story evidence.
Design implication: Show date/scope/symptom context and “what remains unexplained,” with clinician-reviewed safety net.

Principle: Minimize alert fatigue by making alerts actionable, ranked and owned.
Why it matters: Clinician evidence documents EHR alert overload, fatigue and cognitive load.
Design implication: Few high-signal alerts, duplicate suppression, task worklists, workload monitoring, false-positive tracking.

Principle: Configure the product by care-flow model and resource level.
Why it matters: Global evidence shows gatekeeper, open-access, integrated, fragmented, CHW-based, rural, migrant and Indigenous workflows differ.
Design implication: Do not assume one EHR, one GP owner, broadband, interpreters, or specialist access; support offline/paper/SMS paths.

Principle: Expose uncertainty clearly and never present the system as a final diagnostic authority.
Why it matters: Safety notes across packages warn against false reassurance, overtesting and replacing clinical judgment.
Design implication: Use support language, source links, clinician review, override rationale and false positive/negative monitoring.

Why it matters: Poor handoffs recur in ED, inpatient, referral, discharge and public/private transitions.
Design implication: Structured handoff payload: diagnosis/uncertainty, pending tasks, watch-outs, receiving acknowledgment and next owner.

Principle: Design for trust, language, culture and access barriers as diagnostic inputs.
Why it matters: Global evidence shows language, racism, cultural safety, cost, transport and digital exclusion affect whether symptoms are voiced, believed and followed up.
Design implication: Interpreter/cultural-safety workflows, access barriers, patient-held records, liaison routing, and non-punitive resource documentation.
